pattern: Two-pass flood-fill: first erase all land reachable from any border cell (those cannot form closed islands), then count the remaining connected land regions as closed islands.
hints: A land group touching any border cell can never be closed — it has an escape route. Flood-fill outward from every border land cell (0) first, marking each visited cell so it won't be counted later.

After the border-connected land is neutralised, scan the interior: each unvisited land cell (0) you encounter is the start of a closed island — flood-fill it to claim the whole region, then increment your counter.

The flood-fill itself is straightforward DFS or BFS in four cardinal directions; the key insight is that the two-pass order (erase border-land first, count interior second) lets you reuse the same fill logic.

constraints.note: m rows, n cols, each dimension in 1..100; 0 = land, 1 = water

edge_inputs.description: all water — no land at all, zero closed islands
all land — every cell touches the border, so zero closed islands
land cell touches the border on one side — not closed
single interior land cell fully surrounded by water — one closed island
first example — two distinct closed regions
second example — one closed island, border-touching land eliminated
third example — concentric ring of water with two interior islands
1x1 grid with a single land cell — it is the border itself, so not closed
two adjacent interior land cells form one closed island

stress.description: large grid with random land/water — checks correctness under scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    # Force border to water so stress focuses on interior island counting
    for r in range(n):
        grid[r][0] = 1
        grid[r][n - 1] = 1
    for c in range(n):
        grid[0][c] = 1
        grid[n - 1][c] = 1
    return [grid]
